content.text: Data in LLMOps is not a single artefact — it is a family of at least five distinct data types that each play a different role at a different lifecycle stage. Treating them as interchangeable leads to compliance failures, quality regressions, and wasted compute. Understanding each type, how it is produced, and how it must be governed is the first step in designing a sound LLMOps data layer.
Web text, books, code, scientific papers
Curated (input, output) pairs in the target style/domain
Quality control, label consistency, PII scrubbing
Fixed (input, expected-output) pairs with human labels
Stability — never silently change expected outputs
Access control, staleness, embedding model pin
Real (prompt, completion, metadata) logs from live traffic

For teams using API-hosted foundation models this type is handled by the provider, but understanding it matters for evaluating provider claims and for teams training their own models. The pre-training corpus is vast (trillions of tokens), multi-source, and legally complex. Key tasks at this layer:

: near-duplicate web pages inflate perceived diversity, cause memorisation of repeated text, and skew model behaviour toward over-represented sources. MinHash or suffix-array deduplication is applied before training.
: heuristic filters (perplexity threshold, language ID, length constraints) remove low-quality documents.
: remove documents from sources that have opted out via robots.txt or TDM opt-out registries before training.

A fine-tuning dataset is a curated collection of (instruction, response) pairs that teaches the model to behave in a specific way: follow a particular style, answer domain-specific questions, produce a constrained output format. Quality is more important than quantity: 1,000 carefully curated examples outperform 100,000 noisy ones.
Experts write ideal responses from scratch
Highest quality; expensive; use for complex reasoning or safety-critical tasks
Model generates candidates; humans rank or edit
Good quality at lower cost; used by most RLHF pipelines
Stronger model generates responses; weaker model fine-tuned on them
Fast and cheap; legal risk if provider ToS prohibits use for training
Real user queries + curated/approved responses from prod
Maximally representative; requires PII scrubbing and consent framework
The golden set is covered in depth in the Eval-Centric Ops sub-subtopic. From a data governance perspective, the critical property is

: the inputs in a golden set must never change silently. If the expected output changes (because the product spec changed), that change must be logged with a reason, reviewed, and committed explicitly. Accidental drift in the golden set destroys the regression signal.
Store golden-set files in a dedicated directory under version control. Add a CI check that fails if any golden-set input is modified in a PR without an explicit 'golden-set-change: yes' label.

For RAG-based applications, the retrieval corpus is the live knowledge base that augments inference. It has different lifecycle characteristics from training data: it can be updated independently of the model, it must be chunked and embedded before queries can retrieve from it, and it has access-control requirements that training data does not.

Chunking determines the granularity of retrieval. A chunk that is too large retrieves context that is not relevant to the query; one that is too small loses surrounding context needed for coherent answers. Three strategies are in common use:
Split at N tokens with M-token overlap
Split at natural sentence or paragraph breaks
Preserves semantic units; better for structured documents
Try larger chunks first; subdivide only if chunk exceeds token limit

def chunk_by_sentences(text: str, max_tokens: int = 256,
                        overlap_sentences: int = 1) -> list[str]:
    """
    Split text into overlapping sentence-based chunks.
    Uses a simple sentence splitter — use spaCy for production.
    """
    import re
    sentences = re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])\s+', text.strip())
    chunks, current, current_len = [], [], 0

    for sent in sentences:
        sent_len = len(sent.split())
        if current_len + sent_len > max_tokens and current:
            chunks.append(" ".join(current))
            # Overlap: keep last N sentences
            current = current[-overlap_sentences:]
            current_len = sum(len(s.split()) for s in current)
        current.append(sent)
        current_len += sent_len

    if current:
        chunks.append(" ".join(current))
    return chunks

sample = ("Transformers use attention to model relationships between tokens. "
          "The attention mechanism computes a weighted sum of values based on query-key similarity. "
          "Multi-head attention runs multiple attention operations in parallel. "
          "This allows the model to attend to information from different representation subspaces.")

chunks = chunk_by_sentences(sample, max_tokens=30, overlap_sentences=1)
for i, c in enumerate(chunks):
    print(f"Chunk {i}: {c[:80]}...")

The retrieval corpus must be embedded with a specific model version, and the same model must be used at query time. Changing the embedding model without re-embedding the corpus produces incorrect distance comparisons — a silent, hard-to-debug regression. Pin the embedding model version alongside the LLM version in the model registry.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class RetrievalCorpusMetadata:
    corpus_name: str
    document_count: int
    chunk_count: int
    embedding_model: str        # exact model identifier, e.g. "text-embedding-3-small"
    embedding_dim: int
    chunking_strategy: str
    last_updated: str           # ISO 8601
    access_groups: list[str]    # document-level ACL groups

    def is_compatible_with(self, query_embedding_model: str) -> bool:
        """
        Query and index must use the same embedding model.
        Return False if they differ — caller should re-embed or reject query.
        """
        return self.embedding_model == query_embedding_model

Production traces are the logs of every real interaction with the deployed system: the system prompt, the user message, the model response, latency, token counts, and any evaluation scores applied post-hoc. Traces are the most valuable and the most legally sensitive data type in LLMOps.
Join traces to eval results, cost reports, incident investigations
Know exactly which system produced the output
Cost attribution; anomaly detection on prompt length
De-duplicate; detect abuse patterns; avoid logging PII
Needed for eval and fine-tuning; highest privacy risk

Default to logging hashes, not plaintext, for user messages. Log plaintext only with explicit user consent and under a clearly defined retention and access policy. Plaintext logs are a data breach waiting to happen.
Production traces feed back into the fine-tuning dataset — the 'data flywheel' that continuously improves the model on real-world inputs. The flywheel has four steps:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class TraceStatus(Enum):
    RAW           = "raw"           # logged, no further processing
    PII_SCRUBBED  = "pii_scrubbed"  # PII removed; safe to review
    LABELLED      = "labelled"      # human or judge quality label assigned
    APPROVED      = "approved"      # approved for inclusion in fine-tuning dataset
    REJECTED      = "rejected"      # quality below threshold; do not use

@dataclass
class ProductionTrace:
    request_id: str
    model_version: str
    prompt_version: str
    user_message_hash: str
    user_message: str | None        # None if not consented
    response: str | None            # None if not consented
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    ttft_ms: float
    e2e_ms: float
    judge_score: float | None = None
    status: TraceStatus = TraceStatus.RAW
    labels: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

def flywheel_pipeline(traces: list[ProductionTrace]) -> dict:
    """
    Select high-quality traces for inclusion in next fine-tuning dataset.
    Returns (approved_traces, rejection_summary).
    """
    approved, rejected = [], []
    for t in traces:
        # Step 1: require explicit response logging
        if t.response is None:
            rejected.append((t.request_id, "no_response_consent"))
            continue
        # Step 2: quality gate (judge score or labeller approval)
        if t.judge_score is not None and t.judge_score < 0.7:
            rejected.append((t.request_id, "low_quality"))
            continue
        if t.status not in (TraceStatus.APPROVED, TraceStatus.LABELLED):
            rejected.append((t.request_id, "not_reviewed"))
            continue
        approved.append(t)

    return {
        "approved": len(approved),
        "rejected": len(rejected),
        "flywheel_yield": len(approved) / len(traces) if traces else 0,
    }
Every model trained or evaluated in production should be able to answer these questions about its data:
Which version of the fine-tuning dataset was used?
Which version of the eval golden set was used for validation?
What was the retrieval corpus version at deployment time?
Which production trace cohort seeded the next fine-tuning cycle?
The minimal tooling for answering these is a data lineage record attached to each model version in the model registry:

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ModelDataLineage:
    model_version: str
    base_model_pin: str
    fine_tuning_dataset_version: str | None   # None if no fine-tuning
    eval_golden_set_version: str
    retrieval_corpus_version: str | None      # None if no RAG
    prod_trace_cohort: str | None             # trace date range used as flywheel seed

    def summary(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"Model:         {self.model_version}",
            f"Base:          {self.base_model_pin}",
            f"Fine-tune data:{self.fine_tuning_dataset_version or 'none'}",
            f"Golden set:    {self.eval_golden_set_version}",
            f"Retrieval:     {self.retrieval_corpus_version or 'none'}",
            f"Trace seed:    {self.prod_trace_cohort or 'none'}",
        ]
        return "\n".join(lines)

record = ModelDataLineage(
    model_version="support-assistant/v4",
    base_model_pin="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    fine_tuning_dataset_version="support-ft/v2.1",
    eval_golden_set_version="support-eval/v3",
    retrieval_corpus_version="kb-docs/2025-06-01",
    prod_trace_cohort="prod-traces/2025-05",
)
print(record.summary())

LLMOps involves five distinct data types: the pre-training corpus (governed by copyright and opt-out compliance), the fine-tuning dataset (quality-filtered, PII-scrubbed, version-controlled), the eval golden set (stable, never silently changed), the retrieval corpus (chunked, embedded with a pinned model, access-controlled), and production traces (the most legally sensitive, default to hash-logging, feed the data flywheel). Each type requires different collection, quality, and retention practices. Data lineage — recording which data version was used to produce each model version — is the foundation for auditing, debugging, and regulatory compliance.
